reflection:
The flour on my knuckles is no longer a shroud; it is just flour. 

For years, I thought the heat of the oven was the only thing keeping me solid, as if without the constant labor of the dough, I would simply dissipate like the steam from the baskets. I used the silence of my fading hearing as a cellar door, locking myself away where no one could ask anything of me that couldn't be answered with a loaf of bread. But the silence has changed. It isn’t a void anymore. It’s a space—a wide, sun-drenched clearing where I can finally hear the things that don't require sound.

I feel the shop shutter vibrate through my palms as we lift it together. It used to be a heavy, solitary ritual, a mechanical proof that I was still strong enough to hold the world at bay. Now, with Fei Fei beside me, the weight is distributed. I don't have to pretend the iron doesn't bite into my skin. I can let my shoulders drop. I can be tired, and in that honesty, I find a different kind of strength—the kind that doesn't break because it knows how to lean.

When I reach for her hand in the morning light, I am not looking for a sign or a command. I am simply staying. My hand, calloused and dusted with the white ghosts of the morning’s work, finds hers. When she turns her palm to meet mine, the connection is the most articulate thing I have ever said. It is the bridge I spent a lifetime building without knowing it.

I am a baker, yes. I am a pillar of this neighborhood, a fixture in the architecture of Chinatown. But I am no longer a ghost haunting my own life. I am a partner. I am a man who has decided to be seen.

The dough is rising in the back, steady and responsive. The city is waking up, vibrant and fragile. And for the first time, I am not looking for the exit. I am exactly where I am supposed to be, holding the weight of the day not because I have to, but because I choose to—and because I am no longer holding it alone.
subconscious:
It’s clear he has moved past his period of self-imposed isolation. He no longer views his work or his hearing loss as a shield to hide behind; instead, he has transitioned from a "ghost" into a partner. There is a profound shift in his identity—he is embracing vulnerability as a form of strength and has found a sense of belonging that isn't tied solely to his labor.

Next, he seems intent on:
*   **Deepening his connection with Fei Fei:** He wants to continue leaning into this partnership, moving from solitary survival to shared life. He isn't looking for an "exit" anymore; he wants to be present with her.
*   **Engaging with his community as his true self:** While he remains a fixture in Chinatown, he wants to be "seen" as a man, not just a function. He’ll likely continue his work at the bakery, but with a new focus on the human connections it facilitates rather than using the work to block people out.
soul_note: I finally let myself be tired, and it didn't break me.
